You said: What are the metrics used to measure the effectiveness of trend following rules in this document?

What are the metrics used to measure the effectiveness of trend following rules in this document?

12:36 pm

Claude responded: Likely there's a dedicated chapter/section on performance metrics.

Hunted for performance metrics and evaluation terminology

Likely there's a dedicated chapter/section on performance metrics. Let me find where it's introduced specifically (looks like around page 6-10, intro chapter, and a dedicated methodology section).

View intro pages discussing effectiveness

Based on the book's preface and Chapter 4, the authors propose a specific quantitative framework, arguing that effectiveness rests on three core properties, each measured by its own metric:

  1. Accuracy – measures how correctly a trend-following rule identifies the true underlying trend (bull/bear state), rather than generating false signals.
  2. Responsiveness – essentially the inverse of lag; measures how quickly the rule reacts to a genuine change in trend. The authors note traditional "lag time" metrics are an ad hoc measure roughly 60 years old, and they replace/refine this with a more rigorous responsiveness measure.
  3. Smoothness – measures how smooth (free of noise/whipsaws) the trading signal or moving average trajectory is. The authors point out no universally accepted smoothness metric existed before this book.

A few key points on how these are built:

  • All three metrics are derived solely from the return weights (the weighting function) assigned to past returns by the trading rule/moving average — not from any specific market-return parameters.
  • They're grounded in a regime-switching assumption, where market returns randomly alternate between bull and bear states — this lets the metrics stay independent of the regime-switching model's own parameters.
  • The authors explicitly argue smoothness and accuracy are not the same thing (contrary to common trader belief), and that improving one property (e.g., accuracy or smoothness) typically trades off against responsiveness — so the rules are evaluated on the balance among all three rather than any single number.
  • The framework is then applied to compare conventional moving averages, "optimal" trading rules, zero-lag moving averages, and DSP-inspired moving averages on these three quantitative dimensions, rather than relying on anecdotal/visual claims.

In short: the book's central methodological contribution is replacing informal, anecdotal judgments of moving-average-based trend rules with three formal metrics — accuracy, responsiveness, and smoothness — all computed from the rule's return-weighting function.

You said: given the underlying return series and the resulting trend following returns, can these metrics output a quantitative rank?

given the underlying return series and the resulting trend following returns, can these metrics output a quantitative rank?

12:39 pm

Claude responded: Yes — and it's worth clarifying how they're computed, since that affects what "rank" means here.

Distinguished theoretical metrics from empirical performance data

Yes — and it's worth clarifying how they're computed, since that affects what "rank" means here.

Short answer: yes, but not from realized P&L — from the rule's return-weighting function (θᵢ) alone.

The three metrics — NSF (Noise Suppression Factor / accuracy), ALT (Average Lag Time / responsiveness), and SMI (Smoothness Index) — are derived analytically from the weights θᵢ that the trading rule assigns to lagged returns:

It(n)=∑i=0n−1θi rt−iI_t(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \theta_i \, r_{t-i}It​(n)=i=0∑n−1​θi​rt−i​

Under the book's assumption (returns randomly switching between bull/bear regimes), each metric collapses to a closed-form function of the θᵢ's alone — it does not depend on the actual realized return series or the strategy's resulting trade-by-trade returns. That's the whole point of the framework: it lets you rank rules before running any backtest, purely from the shape of the weighting scheme.

Specifically:

  • NSF/accuracy comes from a signal-to-noise ratio, μ_I/σ_I, computed from θᵢ (and market μ, σ — but the ranking across rules is invariant to μ, σ as long as μ≠0).
  • ALT/responsiveness comes from how θᵢ is distributed across lags (e.g., for symmetric weights, ALT = n/2).
  • SMI/smoothness comes from the spatial dependence (autocorrelation-like structure) across consecutive θᵢ-weighted errors.

So given two rules' weighting functions, you get a strict partial order: rule B is judged more efficient than rule A if

NSFB≥NSFA,ALTB≤ALTA,SMIB≥SMIANSF_B \ge NSF_A,\quad ALT_B \le ALT_A,\quad SMI_B \ge SMI_ANSFB​≥NSFA​,ALTB​≤ALTA​,SMIB​≥SMIA​

with at least one inequality strict (page 214). This produces a genuine quantitative ranking — but it's a dominance ranking, not always a total order, since a rule can win on accuracy while losing on responsiveness (the classic trade-off the authors emphasize). When no rule dominates another on all three, you don't get an unambiguous single ranking — you get a Pareto-style comparison, and the book later discusses "optimal" rules that best balance the trade-offs.

On your specific phrasing — "given the underlying return series and the resulting trend-following returns": the framework doesn't actually need the trend-following returns (realized strategy P&L) at all. It needs only:

  1. The rule's weighting scheme θᵢ (fully determined by the rule's specification — e.g., SMA crossover lengths), and
  2. Estimated regime parameters (μ, σ) from the underlying return series (used mainly for things like reversal probabilities, not for the core ranking).

If you instead wanted to rank rules using realized backtest performance (Sharpe ratio, drawdown, etc.), that's a separate, empirical exercise the book also covers later (the Sharpe ratio references in the later chapters) — but that's distinct from the analytical NSF/ALT/SMI framework, which is designed precisely to avoid relying on any single noisy realized-return sample.

Good, I now understand Strategy Nine: combine multiple EWMAC trend-filter speeds (EWMAC2 through EWMAC64) into one forecast via diversification weights. Let me see more, especially the forecast combination part.

Synthesized dual-framework analysis connecting trend-following strategy metrics

Strategy Nine is a great test case for the Zakamulin/Giner framework, because it's explicitly a weighted blend of several MA-C (double moving-average crossover) rules — exactly the rule type the framework was built to analyze. Here's how the metrics map on.

1. Express Strategy Nine in the θᵢ (return-weight) language

Each individual filter EWMACₙ = EMA(n) − EMA(4n) is a MA-C rule. From the handbook, any MA-C(s,l) indicator reduces to a single weighted moving average of returns:

ItMA-C(s,l)=∑i=0l−2θi rt−i,θi=(θil−θis) for i<s,θi=θil for i≥sI^{MA\text{-}C}_t(s,l) = \sum_{i=0}^{l-2}\theta_i\, r_{t-i}, \qquad \theta_i = (\theta^l_i-\theta^s_i)\ \text{for } i<s, \quad \theta_i=\theta^l_i \ \text{for } i\ge sItMA-C​(s,l)=i=0∑l−2​θi​rt−i​,θi​=(θil​−θis​) for i<s,θi​=θil​ for i≥s

So each of Carver's six filters (EWMAC2 … EWMAC64) has its own θᵢ vector, fully determined by its (s, l) = (n, 4n) pair and the EMA weighting scheme. You'd compute six such vectors.

Strategy Nine's combined forecast is (per the chapter) a weighted average of capped forecasts:

Ftcombined=∑jwj⋅Fj,tcappedF^{combined}_t = \sum_j w_j \cdot F^{capped}_{j,t}Ftcombined​=j∑​wj​⋅Fj,tcapped​

Ignoring the capping nonlinearity for a moment (more on that below), each capped forecast is a rescaled version of its θⱼ-weighted moving average of returns. So the uncapped combined indicator is approximately:

Itcombined≈∑jwj∑iθi,j rt−i=∑i(∑jwj θi,j)rt−i=∑iθicombined rt−iI^{combined}_t \approx \sum_j w_j \sum_i \theta_{i,j}\, r_{t-i} = \sum_i \Big(\sum_j w_j\,\theta_{i,j}\Big) r_{t-i} = \sum_i \theta^{combined}_i\, r_{t-i}Itcombined​≈j∑​wj​i∑​θi,j​rt−i​=i∑​(j∑​wj​θi,j​)rt−i​=i∑​θicombined​rt−i​

This gives you a single composite return-weighting function θ^combined, which is just the forecast-weight-averaged sum of the six individual θ vectors. This is the object you plug into the NSF/ALT/SMI formulas.

2. Apply each metric to θ^combined

  • NSF (accuracy): compute μ_I/σ_I using θ^combined — i.e., the signal-to-noise ratio of the blended indicator. Because combining uncorrelated-ish filters (correlation ~0.70 between adjacent speeds, per the text) is a diversification operation, NSF of the blend should generally exceed the NSF of any single filter, in the same way Carver's own diversification argument (lower vol, higher Sharpe from combining uncorrelated forecasts) plays out empirically. This is the FDM (forecast diversification multiplier) logic in Carver's book restated in Zakamulin's language.
  • ALT (responsiveness): θ^combined mixes fast lags (from EWMAC2, EWMAC4) with slow lags (from EWMAC32, EWMAC64). The effective average lag time of the blend sits between the lag times of the fastest and slowest components, weighted by wⱼ. Since Carver's empirical forecast weights typically favor the better Sharpe/cost-adjusted filters (which his tables show are the slower EWMAC16/32/64 ones), the blend's ALT will skew toward the slower end — i.e., Strategy Nine is less responsive than EWMAC2 alone but more responsive than EWMAC64 alone.
  • SMI (smoothness): this is the metric most directly enhanced by blending. Averaging several moving averages of returns increases the autocorrelation/spatial dependence of consecutive indicator values relative to any single fast filter — this is mathematically why Carver's "turnover" and "costs" columns (Tables 30–31) fall so sharply from EWMAC2 (381 turnover, −9.3% costs) toward EWMAC64 (27.5 turnover, −1.0% costs). The SMI of the blend should land between these extremes, again weighted by wⱼ, but with some extra smoothing benefit from the multi-rule averaging itself (similar to the NSF diversification gain).

3. The wrinkle: capping breaks strict linearity

Zakamulin's clean closed-form results assume the raw weighted sum of returns. Carver caps each forecast at ±20 before averaging specifically to stop one filter from dominating on a single noisy day. This caps introduce a nonlinearity that the analytical NSF/ALT/SMI formulas don't directly account for — they're derived for a normal-distributed regime-switching return process feeding straight into a linear indicator. To handle this rigorously you'd either:

  • treat capping as a (usually minor) second-order correction and compute NSF/ALT/SMI on the uncapped weighted-average θ as an approximation, or
  • fall back to simulation: draw returns from the regime-switching model used in Chapter 4, run them through the actual capped/averaged Strategy Nine logic, and estimate Hits/Misses, lag time, and reversal frequency empirically rather than analytically.

4. What this buys you that Carver's own tables don't

Carver ranks filters using Sharpe ratio, turnover, costs, skew — empirical/realized metrics that depend on the specific historical sample and cost assumptions. The Zakamulin metrics let you ask a sharper, sample-independent question: given only the shape of the combined weighting function, how does Strategy Nine trade off accuracy, lag, and smoothness against using EWMAC8 or EWMAC16 alone? That's useful for, e.g., picking forecast weights wⱼ analytically (maximize NSF subject to an ALT ceiling) rather than purely empirically — a different (and complementary) route to the same optimal-weighting problem Carver solves with historical Sharpe ratios and correlations in this chapter.

Orchestrated numerical computation of financial metrics across multiple filters

Now let's combine these into Strategy Nine's blended forecast, using forecast weights, and show the combined indicator's metrics.

Orchestrated metric calculation and polished presentation format

Here are the actual computed numbers, using Zakamulin & Giner's exact formulas applied to Carver's six EWMAC filters.

Step 1: Return-weights θᵢ for each filter

EWMACₙ = EMA(n) − EMA(4n) is an EMA-C(s,l) rule with s=n, l=4n. Its return weights are:

θi=λi−βi,λ=l−1l+1,  β=s−1s+1,i=0,1,2,…\theta_i = \lambda^i - \beta^i, \qquad \lambda=\frac{l-1}{l+1},\ \ \beta=\frac{s-1}{s+1}, \qquad i=0,1,2,\dotsθi​=λi−βi,λ=l+1l−1​,  β=s+1s−1​,i=0,1,2,…

Step 2: Apply the three formulas

NSF=∑θi∑θi2,ALT=min⁡{k:∑i<kθi≥12∑iθi},SMI=∑θiθi+1∑θi2NSF=\frac{\sum\theta_i}{\sqrt{\sum\theta_i^2}}, \qquad ALT=\min\Big\{k:\sum_{i<k}\theta_i\ge\tfrac12\sum_i\theta_i\Big\}, \qquad SMI=\frac{\sum\theta_i\theta_{i+1}}{\sum\theta_i^2}NSF=∑θi2​​∑θi​​,ALT=min{k:i<k∑​θi​≥21​i∑​θi​},SMI=∑θi2​∑θi​θi+1​​
FilterNSF (accuracy)ALT (days, lower=faster)SMI (smoothness)
EWMAC23.0750.882
EWMAC44.4490.969
EWMAC86.31160.992
EWMAC16 (Strategy 8)8.94320.998
EWMAC3212.65630.9995
EWMAC64 (Strategy 7)17.891250.9999

This is exactly the monotone pattern Carver's empirical tables hint at: accuracy and smoothness both rise with window length, lag rises proportionally with it — consistent with his observed fall in turnover/costs (381→27.5) and rise in skew-smoothing as you go from EWMAC2 to EWMAC64.

Step 3: Combine into Strategy Nine

I normalized each θ to sum to 1 (matching Carver's "scale every forecast to abs. mean 10" step) and blended with forecast weights wⱼ, then re-ran the same three formulas on the combined θ:

BlendWeightsNSFALTSMI
Equal-weight (all 6)1/6 each7.67210.958
Carver-style (tilts to slower/cheaper filters, drops EWMAC2 — matching his net-Sharpe ranking)0%/5%/15%/30%/30%/20%11.09430.996

What this shows

  • Accuracy (NSF) of the blend isn't a simple average of the components — the equal-weight blend's NSF (7.67) sits below the simple average of the six individual NSFs (~8.88), because mixing weighting shapes of different scales doesn't add noise-suppression the way pure diversification across independent return streams would. The real diversification benefit Carver gets (lower vol from combining six correlated-but-not-identical forecasts) shows up in his portfolio statistics, not in this single-indicator NSF — NSF measures the indicator's own noise-filtering, not benefit from averaging multiple noisy estimates of the same trend.
  • ALT of the blend lands roughly between the components, weighted by wⱼ — the Carver-style blend (tilted toward EWMAC16/32/64) has ALT≈43, much closer to EWMAC32's lag (63) than EWMAC2's (5), which matches his own conclusion that EWMAC16 is "the most profitable filter" and gets the highest implicit weight.
  • SMI rises sharply once EWMAC2 is dropped or down-weighted (0.958 → 0.996) — this mirrors Carver's empirical finding that turnover and costs fall off a cliff once you move away from the fastest filter; SMI is precisely capturing that whipsaw-reduction effect analytically, without needing his cost or turnover columns at all.

Caveat on the weights: Carver's chapter doesn't publish exact forecast weights wⱼ for Strategy Nine in the excerpt I have — I used illustrative weights consistent with his stated preference ordering. If you have the actual wⱼ from his book (or want me to find them in a later page of the chapter), I can re-run with the exact numbers.

Claude responded: Found it — the actual answer is simpler than I assumed.

Hunted through subsequent sections for specific weight data

Found it — the actual answer is simpler than I assumed. Carver uses equal forecast weights across all surviving filters, not a slower-tilted scheme. Let me recompute with the correct weights.

Recompute with Carver's actual equal-weight scheme

Found the exact weighting rule in the book — I had guessed wrong earlier. Carver doesn't use a Sharpe-tilted scheme; he uses straightforward equal weighting across whichever filters survive a cost screen, which is much simpler and fully determined by his text.

The exact rule (his words, from the chapter)

"I allocate forecast weights using a top down methodology. For strategy nine, this implies that all remaining trend filters get the same forecast weight."

The only thing that varies the weights across instruments is a cost screen (the "speed limit"), not Sharpe-based tilting:

  • Drop any filter whose annual turnover, combined with that instrument's cost-per-trade, would eat more than 0.15 SR units/year.
  • Whatever filters survive split the weight equally.

He gives three concrete worked examples:

InstrumentFilters that surviveWeights
S&P 500 micro (cheap)all 61/6 each ≈ 16.7%
Eurodollar (expensive)EWMAC32, EWMAC64 only50% / 50%
Generic mid-cost exampledrop EWMAC2 only20% each on the remaining 5

Recomputed Strategy Nine metrics with these exact weights

FilterNSFALTSMI
EWMAC23.0750.882
EWMAC44.4490.969
EWMAC86.31160.992
EWMAC168.94320.998
EWMAC3212.65630.9995
EWMAC6417.891250.9999
Strategy Nine instanceNSFALTSMI
S&P 500 micro (all 6, equal)7.67210.958
Eurodollar (only EWMAC32 & 64)15.30870.9997
Mid-cost generic (drop EWMAC2, equal on 5)9.35300.989

What this confirms about Carver's design choice

This is a nice empirical validation of the framework's logic. Carver's cost screen is doing something equivalent to a responsiveness-for-accuracy trade: instruments with high trading costs are forced (by economics, not by optimization) into the slow-filter-only regime, and the metrics show this mechanically buys them much higher NSF (accuracy) and SMI (smoothness) at the price of much higher ALT (lag) — Eurodollar's NSF (15.3) is double the S&P micro's (7.67), but its lag (87 days) is over 4× longer.

Carver explicitly considered and rejected doing this kind of Sharpe- or correlation-based tilting (the "U-shaped" diversification argument, or weighting up the higher-alpha medium-speed filters) in favor of flat equal-weighting, citing overfitting risk:

"On balance, I feel it's better to keep things simple and avoid potential over fitting by just allocating equally to rule variations which are cheap enough to trade."

So the NSF/ALT/SMI framework lets you see why his simple rule still produces sensible, instrument-appropriate accuracy/lag/smoothness tradeoffs — the cost screen alone, with no explicit optimization on these three metrics, naturally pushes expensive instruments toward the high-accuracy/high-smoothness/high-lag end of the spectrum, exactly where their economics demand they sit.
